part_title: Section Eight: Biological Control Using Insect Parasitoids and Predators
part_short_title: Biological Control Using Insect Parasitoids and Predators
part_subtitle: Biological Control Using Parasitoids and Predators

items.text: Chapter Ten
Integrated Management of Tuta absoluta
Introduction

Biological control is a fundamental component of sustainable management of Tuta absoluta. It relies on natural enemies capable of attacking eggs, larvae, pupae, or adults, thereby reducing pest survival, reproduction, and carryover into subsequent generations.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Biological control is a fundamental component of sustainable management of Tuta absoluta. It relies on [[term:biological-control]]natural enemies[[/term]] capable of attacking eggs, larvae, [[term:pupation]]pupae[[/term]], or adults, thereby reducing pest survival, reproduction, and carryover into subsequent generations.

items.text: Biological control does not merely mean releasing a beneficial insect into the field. It is a scientific system that requires:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Biological control does not merely mean releasing a beneficial insect into the field. It is a scientific system that requires:

items: Correct identification of the natural enemy.
Knowledge of the pest stage it attacks.
Assessment of its searching ability and capacity to locate the host.
Determination of suitable temperature and humidity conditions.
Confirmation of compatibility with pesticides used.
Selection of release timing and method.
Measurement of establishment and reproduction within the crop.
Evaluation of its effect on pest density and fruit infestation.
Assessment of the economic feasibility of the program.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Knowledge of the pest stage it attacks.
[[prepared]]Assessment of its searching ability and capacity to locate the host.
[[prepared]]Determination of suitable temperature and humidity conditions.
[[prepared]]Measurement of establishment and reproduction within the crop.
[[prepared]]Evaluation of its effect on pest density and fruit infestation.
[[prepared]]Assessment of the economic feasibility of the program.

items.text: Biological control is particularly important for Tuta absoluta because:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Biological control is particularly important for Tuta absoluta because:

items: Eggs are laid on plant surfaces accessible to egg parasitoids.
Larvae remain inside mines that some parasitoids and predators can detect.
Several generalist predators can consume eggs and newly hatched larvae.
Multiple generations allow natural enemies to reproduce concurrently with the pest.
The pest rapidly develops resistance to insecticides.
There is a need to reduce pesticide residues in fresh-market and processing tomatoes.
Natural enemies can be integrated with pheromones, microbial products, and selective insecticides.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Eggs are laid on plant surfaces accessible to egg parasitoids.
[[prepared]]Larvae remain inside [[term:mines]]mines[[/term]] that some parasitoids and predators can detect.
[[prepared]]Several generalist predators can consume eggs and newly hatched larvae.
[[prepared]]Multiple generations allow natural enemies to reproduce concurrently with the pest.
[[prepared]]The pest rapidly develops resistance to insecticides.
[[prepared]]There is a need to reduce pesticide residues in fresh-market and processing tomatoes.
[[prepared]]Natural enemies can be integrated with [[term:pheromone-trap]]pheromones[[/term]], microbial products, and selective insecticides.

items.text: However, biological-control efficacy is not constant under all conditions. Results are affected by monitoring data, initial pest density, release timing, climate, cultivar, crop-management practices, immigration from neighboring fields, and pesticides used.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]However, biological-control efficacy is not constant under all conditions. Results are affected by monitoring data, initial pest density, release timing, climate, cultivar, crop-management practices, immigration from neighboring fields, and pesticides used.

items.text: Definition of a Natural Enemy
A natural enemy is an organism that attacks, consumes, or uses a pest to complete its life cycle, thereby reducing pest abundance.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A natural enemy is an organism that attacks, consumes, or uses a pest to complete its life cycle, thereby reducing pest abundance.

items.text: Natural enemies of Tuta absoluta include:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Natural enemies of Tuta absoluta include:

items.text: This section is limited to insect parasitoids and predators. Entomopathogenic bacteria, fungi, and nematodes are discussed in the following section.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This section is limited to insect parasitoids and predators. Entomopathogenic bacteria, fungi, and nematodes are discussed in the following section.

items.text: Difference Between a Parasitoid and a Predator
Parasitoid
Parasitoid
A parasitoid is an insect whose adult lays eggs inside or on the surface of an insect host. The immature stage then feeds on the host and usually kills it by the end of development.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A parasitoid is an insect whose adult lays eggs inside or on the surface of an insect host. The immature stage then feeds on the host and usually kills it by the end of development.

items.text: A parasitoid differs from a true parasite in that it:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A parasitoid differs from a true parasite in that it:

items: Usually requires one host to complete the development of one individual.
Kills the host by the end of its development.
Searches for a specific pest stage.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Usually requires one host to complete the development of one individual.
[[prepared]]Kills the host by the end of its development.

items.text: Predator
Predator
A predator is an organism that attacks and consumes several prey individuals during its lifetime.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A predator is an organism that attacks and consumes several prey individuals during its lifetime.

items.text: A predator is characterized by the fact that it:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A predator is characterized by the fact that it:

items: Kills more than one pest individual.
May feed on more than one pest species.
Requires suitable food availability for establishment and reproduction.
May partially feed on plants in some species.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]May feed on more than one pest species.
[[prepared]]Requires suitable food availability for establishment and reproduction.
[[prepared]]May partially feed on plants in some species.

items.text: Biological-Control Strategies
Biological control is divided into four principal approaches.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Biological control is divided into four principal approaches.

items.text: Natural Biological Control
This is the reduction caused by naturally occurring natural enemies without deliberate releases.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This is the reduction caused by naturally occurring natural enemies without deliberate releases.

items.text: It includes:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It includes:

items.text: Its importance varies among fields according to pesticide intensity, plant diversity, and climatic conditions.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Its importance varies among fields according to pesticide intensity, plant diversity, and climatic conditions.

items.text: Conservation Biological Control
Conservation Biological Control
This aims to protect existing natural enemies and increase their capacity to function.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This aims to protect existing natural enemies and increase their capacity to function.

items.text: It includes:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It includes:

items: Avoiding unnecessary removal of parasitized eggs.
Avoiding tillage or pruning that removes large numbers of natural enemies at sensitive times.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Avoiding tillage or pruning that removes large numbers of natural enemies at sensitive times.

items.text: This strategy is particularly suitable for open fields because insect releases may be affected by dispersal, immigration, heat, and wind.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This strategy is particularly suitable for open fields because insect releases may be affected by dispersal, immigration, heat, and wind.

items.text: Inoculative Release
Inoculative Release
Relatively small numbers of a natural enemy are released early in the season to establish a population that reproduces within the crop and provides extended control.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Relatively small numbers of a natural enemy are released early in the season to establish a population that reproduces within the crop and provides extended control.

items.text: This approach is especially used with:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This approach is especially used with:

items: Some larval parasitoids capable of establishment.

items.text: Release should begin early because the predator or parasitoid requires time to reproduce and reach an effective density.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Release should begin early because the predator or parasitoid requires time to reproduce and reach an effective density.

items.text: Inundative Release
Inundative Release
Large and repeated numbers are released to achieve rapid suppression without necessarily relying on permanent establishment.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Large and repeated numbers are released to achieve rapid suppression without necessarily relying on permanent establishment.

items.text: This approach is commonly used with:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This approach is commonly used with:

items.text: Under this system, the natural enemy is treated as a control agent requiring a dose, timing, and repeat interval, but it remains a living organism affected by temperature, pesticides, and handling quality.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Under this system, the natural enemy is treated as a control agent requiring a dose, timing, and repeat interval, but it remains a living organism affected by temperature, pesticides, and handling quality.

items.text: Classical Biological Control
Classical Biological Control
This involves introducing a natural enemy from the pest’s native range to establish it permanently in the invaded region.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This involves introducing a natural enemy from the pest’s native range to establish it permanently in the invaded region.

items.text: It requires:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It requires:

items.text: A parasitoid or predator should not be moved between countries or a non-native species released without formal assessment because the natural enemy itself may affect non-target species or interfere with local natural enemies.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A parasitoid or predator should not be moved between countries or a non-native species released without formal assessment because the natural enemy itself may affect non-target species or interfere with local natural enemies.

items.text: Criteria for Selecting a Natural Enemy
A species should not be adopted as a biological-control agent merely because it attacks Tuta absoluta in the laboratory.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A species should not be adopted as a biological-control agent merely because it attacks Tuta absoluta in the laboratory.

items.text: The following should be evaluated:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The following should be evaluated:

items: Ability to reach eggs or larvae on or within the plant.
Thermal compatibility with the production season.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Ability to reach eggs or larvae on or within the plant.

items.text: The strain, not only the species, should be selected because strains of the same species may differ in:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The strain, not only the species, should be selected because strains of the same species may differ in:

items.text: Targeted Pest Stages
No species targets all stages with equal efficacy. Integration is achieved when natural enemies targeting different stages of the pest life cycle are used together.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]No species targets all stages with equal efficacy. Integration is achieved when natural enemies targeting different stages of the pest life cycle are used together.

items.text: First: Egg Parasitoids
Importance of Targeting Eggs
Eggs are a strategic control stage because killing the embryo before hatching prevents:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Eggs are a strategic control stage because killing the embryo before hatching prevents:

items: Movement of larvae into growing points and fruits.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Movement of larvae into growing points and fruits.

items.text: Egg parasitoids act before larvae become protected inside plant tissues.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Egg parasitoids act before larvae become protected inside plant tissues.

items.text: However, their success requires:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]However, their success requires:

items: Synchrony between parasitoid presence and oviposition.
Ability to search among plant trichomes.

items.text: The Genus Trichogramma
The genus Trichogramma includes minute wasps that parasitize lepidopteran eggs.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The genus Trichogramma includes minute wasps that parasitize lepidopteran eggs.

items.text: The female lays one or more eggs inside the pest egg, and the parasitoid larva feeds on its contents, preventing the Tuta larva from hatching.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The female lays one or more eggs inside the pest egg, and the parasitoid larva feeds on its contents, preventing the Tuta larva from hatching.

items.text: Parasitized eggs generally darken as parasitoid development progresses, and the adult parasitoid emerges through a small circular exit hole.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Parasitized eggs generally darken as parasitoid development progresses, and the adult parasitoid emerges through a small circular exit hole.

items.text: Species studied against Tuta absoluta include:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Species studied against Tuta absoluta include:

items.text: Species and strains differ in efficacy. They should therefore be evaluated using Tuta absoluta eggs on tomato plants, not only on eggs of the factitious rearing host.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Species and strains differ in efficacy. They should therefore be evaluated using Tuta absoluta eggs on tomato plants, not only on eggs of the factitious rearing host.

items.text: Trichogramma achaeae
Trichogramma achaeae is among the best-known egg parasitoids studied against Tuta absoluta, particularly in Mediterranean greenhouse programs.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Trichogramma achaeae is among the best-known egg parasitoids studied against Tuta absoluta, particularly in Mediterranean greenhouse programs.

items.text: Important characteristics include:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Important characteristics include:

items: Capacity for production on factitious hosts.
Compatibility with predators, pheromones, and microbial products when properly managed.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Compatibility with predators, pheromones, and microbial products when properly managed.

items.text: A Spanish greenhouse trial recorded a high reduction in damage using an experimental release rate of approximately:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A Spanish greenhouse trial recorded a high reduction in damage using an experimental release rate of approximately:

items.text: 75 adults per square meter every 3–4 days.
This is approximately equivalent to:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This is approximately equivalent to:

items.text: 750,000 individuals per hectare per release.
Or approximately:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Or approximately:

items.text: 315,000 individuals per feddan per release.
However, this rate formed part of a specific greenhouse experiment under particular temperature and plant-density conditions and does not represent a general commercial rate for open-field tomatoes.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]However, this rate formed part of a specific greenhouse experiment under particular temperature and plant-density conditions and does not represent a general commercial rate for open-field tomatoes.

items.text: The practical rate should be determined according to:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The practical rate should be determined according to:

items.text: Importance of the Strain
Two strains of T. achaeae may differ in:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Two strains of T. achaeae may differ in:

items.text: Other Trichogramma species may outperform T. achaeae in specific tests.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Other Trichogramma species may outperform T. achaeae in specific tests.

items.text: Species name alone should therefore not be regarded as a guarantee of efficacy. The following should be known:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Species name alone should therefore not be regarded as a guarantee of efficacy. The following should be known:

items.text: Age of Tuta Eggs
Most egg parasitoids prefer relatively young eggs because their contents are more suitable for parasitoid development.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Most egg parasitoids prefer relatively young eggs because their contents are more suitable for parasitoid development.

items.text: Suitability generally declines as embryonic development advances and hatching approaches.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Suitability generally declines as embryonic development advances and hatching approaches.

items.text: Releases should therefore coincide with:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Releases should therefore coincide with:

items.text: Late releases provide no benefit against eggs that have already hatched and larvae that have entered the plant.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Late releases provide no benefit against eggs that have already hatched and larvae that have entered the plant.

items.text: Effect of Temperature
Temperature affects:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Temperature affects:

items.text: Experiments with T. achaeae showed more efficient movement among plants and host finding at moderate to warm temperatures such as 25–30°C than at lower temperatures such as 15°C.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Experiments with T. achaeae showed more efficient movement among plants and host finding at moderate to warm temperatures such as 25–30°C than at lower temperatures such as 15°C.

items.text: This does not mean that all strains share the same thermal range. The strain used should be tested under local conditions.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This does not mean that all strains share the same thermal range. The strain used should be tested under local conditions.

items.text: Excessively high temperature may cause:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Excessively high temperature may cause:

items: Reduced emergence quality from release cards.

items.text: Methods of Releasing Trichogramma
Several release methods are used.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Several release methods are used.

items.text: Cards Containing Parasitized Eggs
Cards contain eggs of a factitious host that have been parasitized and are close to parasitoid emergence.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Cards contain eggs of a factitious host that have been parasitized and are close to parasitoid emergence.

items.text: They should be:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]They should be:

items: Distributed over a large number of points.
Placed outside irrigation and spray paths.
Not concentrated in a few locations.
Installed immediately after receipt or stored according to instructions.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Distributed over a large number of points.
[[prepared]]Installed immediately after receipt or stored according to instructions.

items.text: Release of Adults
Adults may be released directly at distributed points, but this requires:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Adults may be released directly at distributed points, but this requires:

items: Minimizing time spent in the container.

items.text: Cards are easier to transport and distribute, whereas adult release may provide faster action when handling quality is high.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Cards are easier to transport and distribute, whereas adult release may provide faster action when handling quality is high.

items.text: Release Timing
Release should preferably be conducted:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Release should preferably be conducted:

items: Early in the morning or near sunset.
Away from the hottest and driest periods.
At the beginning of egg presence.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Early in the morning or near sunset.
[[prepared]]Away from the hottest and driest periods.

items.text: Timing should not depend only on the day of the week, but also on:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Timing should not depend only on the day of the week, but also on:

items.text: Release Frequency
Trichogramma usually requires repeated releases because:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Trichogramma usually requires repeated releases because:

items: Some parasitoids disperse outside the treated area.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Some parasitoids disperse outside the treated area.

items.text: Releases may be:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Releases may be:

items: Every 3–4 days in intensive experimental programs.
Adjusted according to egg density and weather.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Adjusted according to egg density and weather.

items.text: No fixed interval should be used in every field without egg inspection.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]No fixed interval should be used in every field without egg inspection.

items.text: Evaluation of Egg Parasitism
Apparent parasitism is calculated as:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Apparent parasitism is calculated as:

items.text: Parasitism (%) = Number of parasitized eggs ÷ Total number of eggs examined × 100
The following should be distinguished:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The following should be distinguished:

items.text: Samples should preferably be held under controlled conditions until parasitoid emergence because dark coloration alone may not always be sufficient to confirm identity or successful emergence.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Samples should preferably be held under controlled conditions until parasitoid emergence because dark coloration alone may not always be sufficient to confirm identity or successful emergence.

items.text: Limitations of Egg Parasitoids
Limitations include:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Limitations include:

items: Short period available before egg hatching.
Reduced efficacy under extremely high egg density.
Variation in quality among commercial batches.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Reduced efficacy under extremely high egg density.

items.text: Egg parasitoids should not be used alone after larvae become widespread because they do not affect existing mines.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Egg parasitoids should not be used alone after larvae become widespread because they do not affect existing mines.

items.text: Second: Larval Parasitoids
Importance of Larval Parasitoids
Larval parasitoids target the stage responsible for direct damage.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Larval parasitoids target the stage responsible for direct damage.

items.text: Some species can:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Some species can:

items: Penetrate plant tissue with the ovipositor.
Lay eggs inside or on the larva.
Kill larvae through parasitism or host feeding.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Lay eggs inside or on the larva.
[[prepared]]Kill larvae through parasitism or host feeding.

items.text: Their effect may be greater than the measured parasitism percentage because some females kill larvae through host feeding without laying eggs.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Their effect may be greater than the measured parasitism percentage because some females kill larvae through host feeding without laying eggs.

items.text: Functional Classification of Larval Parasitoids
Ectoparasitoids
Ectoparasitoids
The parasitoid develops externally on the larval host after paralyzing it or stopping its development.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The parasitoid develops externally on the larval host after paralyzing it or stopping its development.

items.text: Examples include:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Examples include:

items.text: Endoparasitoids
Endoparasitoids
The female lays eggs inside the larval host, and the parasitoid develops internally.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The female lays eggs inside the larval host, and the parasitoid develops internally.

items.text: Examples include:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Examples include:

items.text: Idiobionts
Idiobionts
These paralyze the host or arrest its development at the time of parasitism.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]These paralyze the host or arrest its development at the time of parasitism.

items.text: Koinobionts
Koinobionts
The host continues feeding and growing for a period after parasitism before the parasitoid eventually kills it.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The host continues feeding and growing for a period after parasitism before the parasitoid eventually kills it.

items.text: This difference affects the timing of damage reduction because an internally parasitized larva may continue feeding for some time before death.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This difference affects the timing of damage reduction because an internally parasitized larva may continue feeding for some time before death.

items.text: Necremnus tutae
Necremnus tutae is one of the most important ectoparasitoids of Tuta absoluta larvae in the Mediterranean region.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Necremnus tutae is one of the most important ectoparasitoids of Tuta absoluta larvae in the Mediterranean region.

items.text: In some older studies it was reported as:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]In some older studies it was reported as:

items.text: Subsequent taxonomic revisions showed that an important proportion of these records referred to N. tutae.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Subsequent taxonomic revisions showed that an important proportion of these records referred to N. tutae.

items.text: The female searches for larvae inside mines, then:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The female searches for larvae inside mines, then:

items: Penetrates the plant tissue with its ovipositor.
Lays eggs near or on it.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Penetrates the plant tissue with its ovipositor.

items.text: It generally attacks larvae beyond the first instar, and the first larval instar is not its preferred target.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It generally attacks larvae beyond the first instar, and the first [[term:larval-instar]]larval instar[[/term]] is not its preferred target.

items.text: Host Feeding
A female N. tutae can kill some larvae by feeding on their fluids without producing offspring.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A female N. tutae can kill some larvae by feeding on their fluids without producing offspring.

items.text: This means that:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This means that:

items.text: Total mortality caused by the parasitoid may exceed the percentage of larvae from which adult parasitoids emerge.
Evaluation should therefore record:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Evaluation should therefore record:

items: Larvae that died without parasitoid emergence.

items.text: Parasitoid emergence alone should not be used to estimate the full effect.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Parasitoid emergence alone should not be used to estimate the full effect.

items.text: Efficacy of Necremnus tutae
Natural parasitism varies among fields and seasons.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Natural parasitism varies among fields and seasons.

items.text: Studies have recorded:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Studies have recorded:

items: Low or moderate averages in some fields.
High levels reaching 75–80% under favorable local conditions.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Low or moderate averages in some fields.
[[prepared]]High levels reaching 75–80% under favorable local conditions.

items.text: This depends on:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This depends on:

items: Competition with other parasitoids and predators.

items.text: The maximum percentage does not mean that this level is achieved consistently under commercial conditions.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The maximum percentage does not mean that this level is achieved consistently under commercial conditions.

items.text: Bracon nigricans
Bracon nigricans is a gregarious ectoparasitoid of Tuta larvae and some other lepidopteran larvae.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Bracon nigricans is a gregarious ectoparasitoid of Tuta larvae and some other lepidopteran larvae.

items.text: The female usually:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The female usually:

items: Locates the larva inside the mine.
Lays several eggs near or on its surface.
Produces several parasitoid larvae on the same host.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Lays several eggs near or on its surface.
[[prepared]]Produces several parasitoid larvae on the same host.

items.text: It has been recorded naturally in parts of the Mediterranean basin and Africa.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It has been recorded naturally in parts of the Mediterranean basin and Africa.

items.text: Surveys in Kenyan fields recorded parasitism reaching approximately 21% at some locations, with clear variation among fields and environments.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Surveys in Kenyan fields recorded parasitism reaching approximately 21% at some locations, with clear variation among fields and environments.

items.text: Commercial release requires:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Commercial release requires:

items: Evaluation of suitability for local temperatures.

items.text: Dolichogenidea gelechiidivoris
Dolichogenidea gelechiidivoris is a solitary larval endoparasitoid of Tuta absoluta originating from the Americas.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Dolichogenidea gelechiidivoris is a solitary larval endoparasitoid of Tuta absoluta originating from the Americas.

items.text: It is characterized by the fact that it:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It is characterized by the fact that it:

items: Lays an egg inside the larva.
Allows the host to continue developing temporarily.
Later emerges to form a cocoon.
Can track the host within the mine.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Allows the host to continue developing temporarily.
[[prepared]]Can track the host within the mine.

items.text: It has become recorded or established in parts of the Mediterranean region and Africa after moving beyond its native range.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It has become recorded or established in parts of the Mediterranean region and Africa after moving beyond its native range.

items.text: It is considered promising, but its use requires attention to:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It is considered promising, but its use requires attention to:

items: Formal regulation of movement and release.
Possible changes in the local parasitoid community.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Possible changes in the local parasitoid community.

items.text: Competition Among Larval Parasitoids
The same larva may be attacked by more than one parasitoid.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The same larva may be attacked by more than one parasitoid.

items.text: Competition may occur:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Competition may occur:

items: Between an endoparasitoid and an ectoparasitoid.
When one parasitoid kills the host before another completes development.
Through host feeding on an already parasitized larva.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]When one parasitoid kills the host before another completes development.
[[prepared]]Through host feeding on an already parasitized larva.

items.text: An ectoparasitoid that paralyzes the larva may outcompete an endoparasitoid that had previously laid an egg inside it because paralysis stops host growth and alters its resources.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]An ectoparasitoid that paralyzes the larva may outcompete an endoparasitoid that had previously laid an egg inside it because paralysis stops host growth and alters its resources.

items.text: Combining several species does not therefore necessarily produce an additive effect. Outcomes may include:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Combining several species does not therefore necessarily produce an additive effect. Outcomes may include:

items.text: The combination should be tested before inclusion in a joint-release program.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The combination should be tested before inclusion in a joint-release program.

items.text: Other Parasitoids of Tuta Larvae
Important species in different regions include:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Important species in different regions include:

items: Other species of Eulophidae, Braconidae, and Ichneumonidae.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Other species of Eulophidae, Braconidae, and Ichneumonidae.

items.text: Not all records represent agents ready for biological-control use. Some species may be:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Not all records represent agents ready for biological-control use. Some species may be:

items: Recorded from only a single incidental emergence.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Recorded from only a single incidental emergence.

items.text: A distinction must be made between “a parasitoid recorded from the pest” and “a parasitoid suitable for a control program.”
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A distinction must be made between “a parasitoid recorded from the pest” and “a parasitoid suitable for a control program.”

items.text: Third: Predators
Importance of Predators
Predators kill several pest individuals during their lifetime.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Predators kill several pest individuals during their lifetime.

items.text: The principal predators used against Tuta attack:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The principal predators used against Tuta attack:

items: Newly hatched larvae in superficial mines.

items.text: Generalist predators also contribute to the control of:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Generalist predators also contribute to the control of:

items.text: However, their generalist feeding can also lead them to consume other natural enemies or feed on the plant.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]However, their generalist feeding can also lead them to consume other natural enemies or feed on the plant.

items.text: Predatory Bugs of the Family Miridae
Omnivorous mirid bugs are the most important group of predators studied against Tuta absoluta.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Omnivorous mirid bugs are the most important group of predators studied against Tuta absoluta.

items.text: Important species include:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Important species include:

items: Other Dicyphus species and local mirids.

items.text: Macrolophus pygmaeus
Macrolophus pygmaeus is a generalist predator used in protected tomato programs and attacks:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Macrolophus pygmaeus is a generalist predator used in protected tomato programs and attacks:

items.text: Laboratory studies indicate that adults can consume large numbers of Tuta eggs. However, laboratory rates should not be transferred directly to the field because field predation is affected by searching difficulty, climate, and alternative prey.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Laboratory studies indicate that adults can consume large numbers of Tuta eggs. However, laboratory rates should not be transferred directly to the field because field predation is affected by searching difficulty, climate, and alternative prey.

items.text: The predator tends to prefer:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The predator tends to prefer:

items.text: Its ability to reach large larvae deep inside fruits or stems is limited.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Its ability to reach large larvae deep inside fruits or stems is limited.

items.text: Establishment of Macrolophus pygmaeus
Establishment is relatively slow. Release should therefore preferably occur:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Establishment is relatively slow. Release should therefore preferably occur:

items: In the nursery or immediately after transplanting.
With supplementary food when prey is scarce.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]In the nursery or immediately after transplanting.
[[prepared]]With supplementary food when prey is scarce.

items.text: Greenhouse guidance programs have used rates of approximately:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Greenhouse guidance programs have used rates of approximately:

items.text: One individual per square meter at the beginning of the crop.
Some trials have also used releases of approximately:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Some trials have also used releases of approximately:

items.text: 1–2 adults per square meter.
Other programs have used higher repeated releases.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Other programs have used higher repeated releases.

items.text: These rates apply to the greenhouse products and conditions studied and are not fixed rates for open fields.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]These rates apply to the greenhouse products and conditions studied and are not fixed rates for open fields.

items.text: Supplementary Food for Predators
Tuta absoluta alone may not support rapid establishment of M. pygmaeus, particularly when predators are released preventively before pest appearance.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Tuta absoluta alone may not support rapid establishment of M. pygmaeus, particularly when predators are released preventively before pest appearance.

items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Supplementary foods include:

items: Treated Artemia cysts in some programs.
Alternative prey or specialized commercial foods.

items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]However, excessive or poorly distributed food may cause:

items: Aggregation of predators at limited points.

items.text: It should be used according to product instructions and monitoring results.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It should be used according to product instructions and monitoring results.

items.text: Nesidiocoris tenuis
Nesidiocoris tenuis is one of the strongest predators used against Tuta absoluta in warm regions.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Nesidiocoris tenuis is one of the strongest predators used against Tuta absoluta in warm regions.

items.text: It consumes:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It consumes:

items.text: Its capacity to reproduce on tomato and tolerate warm conditions supports establishment in Mediterranean and other warm environments.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Its capacity to reproduce on tomato and tolerate warm conditions supports establishment in Mediterranean and other warm environments.

items.text: It may reduce Tuta eggs more rapidly than some other mirids, but it exhibits more pronounced plant-feeding behavior.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It may reduce Tuta eggs more rapidly than some other mirids, but it exhibits more pronounced plant-feeding behavior.

items.text: Plant Feeding by Nesidiocoris tenuis
N. tenuis is a zoophytophagous predator.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]N. tenuis is a zoophytophagous predator.

items.text: When its density is high or prey is scarce, it may cause:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]When its density is high or prey is scarce, it may cause:

items: Necrotic rings around stems or petioles.

items.text: Damage does not occur to the same degree under all conditions. It is influenced by:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Damage does not occur to the same degree under all conditions. It is influenced by:

items.text: High N. tenuis abundance should not always be regarded as a positive result. Pest suppression must be balanced against potential plant injury.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]High N. tenuis abundance should not always be regarded as a positive result. Pest suppression must be balanced against potential plant injury.

items.text: Management of Risks Associated with Nesidiocoris tenuis
Management includes:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Management includes:

items: Early release at a calculated rate.
Preventing predator numbers from increasing without supervision.
Avoiding sudden removal of most prey with a broad-spectrum insecticide.
Selecting a selective material if predator reduction becomes necessary.
Avoiding repeated supplementary releases without data.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Preventing predator numbers from increasing without supervision.
[[prepared]]Avoiding sudden removal of most prey with a broad-spectrum insecticide.
[[prepared]]Selecting a selective material if predator reduction becomes necessary.

items.text: A distinction should be made among:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A distinction should be made among:

items.text: Preplant Release
Programs have been developed for releasing N. tenuis onto transplants before transfer to the greenhouse, providing time for predator reproduction before pest arrival.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Programs have been developed for releasing N. tenuis onto transplants before transfer to the greenhouse, providing time for predator reproduction before pest arrival.

items.text: This system requires:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This system requires:

items: Avoidance of insecticides toxic to the predator.
Inspection of transplants for excessive plant damage.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Avoidance of insecticides toxic to the predator.
[[prepared]]Inspection of transplants for excessive plant damage.

items.text: The approach is more controllable in greenhouses than in open fields.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The approach is more controllable in greenhouses than in open fields.

items.text: Dicyphus errans
Dicyphus errans is a generalist omnivorous predator that can consume Tuta absoluta eggs and larvae.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Dicyphus errans is a generalist omnivorous predator that can consume Tuta absoluta eggs and larvae.

items.text: Potential advantages include:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Potential advantages include:

items: Ability to develop on multiple plant species.
Potential use in some European and Mediterranean environments.
Lower expression of plant damage than some other species under particular conditions.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Ability to develop on multiple plant species.
[[prepared]]Potential use in some European and Mediterranean environments.
[[prepared]]Lower expression of plant damage than some other species under particular conditions.

items.text: Its commercial and operational use is less widespread than that of M. pygmaeus and N. tenuis. Release programs require further calibration according to climate and crop.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Its commercial and operational use is less widespread than that of M. pygmaeus and N. tenuis. Release programs require further calibration according to climate and crop.

items.text: Orius spp.
Orius species attack a range of small prey, and some have been recorded feeding on Tuta absoluta eggs.

items.text: Intraguild Predation
Intraguild Predation
Intraguild predation occurs when one natural enemy kills another natural enemy while both attack the same pest.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Intraguild predation occurs when one natural enemy kills another natural enemy while both attack the same pest.

items.text: Potential examples include:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Potential examples include:

items: A mirid consuming a parasitized egg.
N. tenuis preying on M. pygmaeus nymphs when prey is scarce.
An ectoparasitoid killing a larva containing an endoparasitoid.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]N. tenuis preying on M. pygmaeus nymphs when prey is scarce.
[[prepared]]An ectoparasitoid killing a larva containing an endoparasitoid.

items.text: The occurrence of intraguild predation does not mean that combining two species always fails. The overall effect on the pest may remain positive, particularly when prey is abundant and species occupy different plant locations.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The occurrence of intraguild predation does not mean that combining two species always fails. The overall effect on the pest may remain positive, particularly when prey is abundant and species occupy different plant locations.

Dolichogenidea gelechiidivoris is an endoparasitoid that allows the host to continue developing temporarily.

Effects of Pesticides
Broad-spectrum pesticides are among the most important constraints on biological control.

Sublethal effects on fecundity, searching, and flight.

Common Errors
Error One: Releasing Natural Enemies After Infestation Reaches the Fruits
Most biological-control agents need to act before outbreak development.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Most biological-control agents need to act before outbreak development.

items.text: Error Two: Selecting a Species by Name Without Evaluating the Strain
Strains differ in efficacy and tolerance.

items.text: Error Three: Counting Cards Without Knowing the Number of Emerging Individuals
Cards may differ greatly in content.

This prevents uniform distribution.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This prevents uniform distribution.

items.text: Error Five: Exposing Trichogramma Cards to Direct Sunlight
This affects parasitoid emergence and survival.

items.text: Error Six: Using an Incompatible Pesticide After Release
This may eliminate the released batch before it performs its function.

items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Parasitoid emergence should be confirmed and mines monitored.

items.text: Error Eight: Releasing a Predator Without Food or Prey
It may fail to establish or increase plant feeding.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It may fail to establish or increase plant feeding.

items.text: Error Nine: Failing to Monitor Plant Injury Caused by Nesidiocoris tenuis
The predator may become damaging at high density.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The predator may become damaging at high density.

items.text: Error Ten: Randomly Combining Predators and Parasitoids
Intraguild predation or competition may occur.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Intraguild predation or competition may occur.

items.text: Error Eleven: Moving a Non-Native Natural Enemy Without Evaluation
This may create environmental or regulatory risks.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This may create environmental or regulatory risks.

items.text: Error Twelve: Evaluating Success by the Number Released
Establishment and effects on the pest and fruit are the important outcomes.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Establishment and effects on the pest and fruit are the important outcomes.

items.text: Error Thirteen: Relying on Laboratory Results Alone
Efficacy may decline on whole plants and under field conditions.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Efficacy may decline on whole plants and under field conditions.

items.text: Error Fourteen: Ignoring Neighboring Fields
Immigration may exceed the capacity of natural enemies.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Immigration may exceed the capacity of natural enemies.

items.text: Error Fifteen: Removing Many Leaves After Predator Release
Predator nymphs and eggs may be removed with the leaves.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Predator nymphs and eggs may be removed with the leaves.

items.text: Practical Priorities for Open-Field Tomatoes in Egypt
Scientific priorities are:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Scientific priorities are:

items: 1. Survey parasitoids and predators naturally present in tomato-production areas.
2. Identify species morphologically and molecularly when necessary.
3. Protect local natural enemies from broad-spectrum insecticides.
4. Do not transfer European greenhouse rates directly to Egyptian open fields.
5. Evaluate local or available Trichogramma strains using Tuta eggs.
6. Begin egg-parasitoid releases when eggs appear, not after larvae become widespread.
7. Distribute release points along field edges and in the interior.
8. Select release times with moderate temperatures.
10. Evaluate predator establishment in open fields.
11. Monitor plant feeding by N. tenuis.
12. Use supportive non-host plants in structured experimental programs.
13. Do not apply incompatible insecticides after release.
14. Use percentage of infested fruits as the final performance indicator.
15. Coordinate programs across production blocks rather than individual fields only.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]1. Survey parasitoids and predators naturally present in tomato-production areas.
[[prepared]]2. Identify species morphologically and molecularly when necessary.
[[prepared]]3. Protect local natural enemies from broad-spectrum insecticides.
[[prepared]]4. Do not transfer European greenhouse rates directly to Egyptian open fields.
[[prepared]]5. Evaluate local or available Trichogramma strains using Tuta eggs.
[[prepared]]6. Begin egg-parasitoid releases when eggs appear, not after larvae become widespread.
[[prepared]]7. Distribute release points along field edges and in the interior.
[[prepared]]8. Select release times with moderate temperatures.
[[prepared]]10. Evaluate predator establishment in open fields.
[[prepared]]11. Monitor plant feeding by N. tenuis.
[[prepared]]12. Use supportive non-host plants in structured experimental programs.
[[prepared]]13. Do not apply incompatible insecticides after release.
[[prepared]]14. Use percentage of infested fruits as the final performance indicator.
[[prepared]]15. Coordinate programs across production blocks rather than individual fields only.

items.text: There is no single scientifically validated Egyptian release rate suitable for all planting seasons, cultivars, and regions. Local rates should be developed through controlled field trials documenting:

Before Planting
Nursery

items: Release predators early when the system is adopted.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Release predators early when the system is adopted.

items.text: After Transplanting

items: Begin Trichogramma releases when eggs are present.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Begin Trichogramma releases when eggs are present.

items.text: Vegetative Growth
Flowering and Fruit Set
Fruit Filling

items: Do not rely only on increasing release rates during a severe outbreak.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Do not rely only on increasing release rates during a severe outbreak.

items.text: After Harvest

items: Conserve natural enemies in the surrounding environment.
Modify the program for the following season.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Conserve natural enemies in the surrounding environment.
[[prepared]]Modify the program for the following season.

items.text: Summary of Section Eight
Biological control of Tuta absoluta relies on egg and larval parasitoids and predators targeting different stages of the pest life cycle.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Biological control of Tuta absoluta relies on egg and larval parasitoids and predators targeting different stages of the pest life cycle.

items.text: Trichogramma species are the principal egg parasitoids. T. achaeae is among the most studied species in Mediterranean greenhouses, although its efficacy varies among strains, temperatures, release rates, and release timing.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Trichogramma species are the principal egg parasitoids. T. achaeae is among the most studied species in Mediterranean greenhouses, although its efficacy varies among strains, temperatures, release rates, and release timing.

items.text: Egg parasitoids provide the greatest value when released in synchrony with oviposition, before larvae enter leaves and fruits.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Egg parasitoids provide the greatest value when released in synchrony with oviposition, before larvae enter leaves and fruits.

items.text: Important larval parasitoids include:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Important larval parasitoids include:

items.text: The efficacy of larval parasitoids should not be measured only by adult parasitoid emergence because some species cause additional mortality through host paralysis and host feeding.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The efficacy of larval parasitoids should not be measured only by adult parasitoid emergence because some species cause additional mortality through host paralysis and host feeding.

items.text: Predatory mirids, especially Macrolophus pygmaeus and Nesidiocoris tenuis, are among the most important predators used in tomato and attack eggs and newly hatched larvae.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Predatory mirids, especially Macrolophus pygmaeus and Nesidiocoris tenuis, are among the most important predators used in tomato and attack eggs and newly hatched larvae.

items.text: N. tenuis has a high predation capacity but may cause plant injury when predator density is high or prey is scarce and therefore requires careful monitoring.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]N. tenuis has a high predation capacity but may cause plant injury when predator density is high or prey is scarce and therefore requires careful monitoring.

items.text: Biological control performs best when:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Biological control performs best when:

items: They are protected from non-selective pesticides.
Suitable climatic and nutritional conditions are available.
Results are measured using fruit infestation and acceptable yield.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Suitable climatic and nutritional conditions are available.
[[prepared]]Results are measured using fruit infestation and acceptable yield.

items.text: There is no single universal release rate for any parasitoid or predator. Rates from experiments or foreign greenhouses should not be converted directly into recommendations for Egyptian open fields without local calibration.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]There is no single universal release rate for any parasitoid or predator. Rates from experiments or foreign greenhouses should not be converted directly into recommendations for Egyptian open fields without local calibration.
